rawOutput:
{
  "topic": "Advanced System Design Mastery",
  "topic_slug": "advanced-system-design-mastery",
  "level": "Advanced / Exhaustive",
  "goal": "Professional mastery through first-principles engineering; building a bottom-up mental model of large-scale systems.",
  "estimated_total_minutes": 4080,
  "modules": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "title": "Hardware & OS Primitives: The Physics of Software",
      "estimated_minutes": 600,
      "description": "Before abstracting to the cloud, you must understand the uncompromising physical constraints of a single machine. This module establishes the absolute bedrock of system performance.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Calculate strict latency budgets and predict system bottlenecks by deeply understanding CPU, memory, and disk I/O primitives at the kernel level.",
        "concepts": [
          "Memory hierarchy and nanosecond-level latency numbers",
          "L1/L2/L3 Cache vs Main Memory vs Disk Seek",
          "Blocking vs. Non-blocking I/O",
          "Synchronous vs. Asynchronous I/O",
          "Kernel-level event loops: epoll (Linux) and io_uring",
          "CPU Concurrency vs. Parallelism",
          "Context switching overhead and Kernel vs. User space transitions",
          "Thread pools and user-space threads (Goroutines/Green threads)",
          "SSD Internals: Page vs. Block writing",
          "Sequential vs. Random I/O economics"
        ],
        "sourceRefs": [
          "Latency Numbers Every Programmer Should Know",
          "Designing Data-Intensive Applications (DDIA): Reliability, Scalability, and Maintainability"
        ],
        "prerequisites": []
      },
      "subtopics": [],
      "generated": false,
      "checkpoint": {
        "type": "quiz",
        "num_questions": 8,
        "pass_threshold": 80,
        "question_types": ["conceptual", "application", "calculation"]
      }
    },
    {
      "id": 2,
      "title": "Network Internals & Transport Layer: The Synapses",
      "estimated_minutes": 480,
      "description": "Distributed systems are bound by the wires that connect them. This module strips away HTTP abstractions to build a first-principles understanding of how bytes actually move between machines.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Architect high-throughput, low-latency communication channels by manipulating transport protocols and serialization formats at the binary level.",
        "concepts": [
          "TCP/IP Deep Dive: 3-way handshake mechanics",
          "TCP Flow Control (Sliding Windows) and Congestion Control (Slow Start/Congestion Avoidance)",
          "UDP fundamentals and limitations",
          "QUIC protocol and HTTP/3: Solving Head-of-Line (HOL) blocking",
          "HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 multiplexing mechanics",
          "Binary Serialization Protocols: Protocol Buffers (Protobuf), Avro, Thrift",
          "Schema evolution in distributed environments",
          "gRPC multiplexing and streaming internals",
          "WebSockets: Managing long-lived bidirectional state",
          "REST: Statelessness and Idempotency guarantees"
        ],
        "sourceRefs": [
          "DDIA: Encoding and Evolution"
        ],
        "prerequisites": [
          "Blocking vs. Non-blocking I/O",
          "Memory hierarchy and nanosecond-level latency numbers"
        ]
      },
      "subtopics": [],
      "generated": false,
      "checkpoint": {
        "type": "quiz",
        "num_questions": 10,
        "pass_threshold": 80,
        "question_types": ["conceptual", "application", "scenario"]
      }
    },
    {
      "id": 3,
      "title": "Storage Engine Mechanics: Data at Rest",
      "estimated_minutes": 720,
      "description": "Data is the only true state in system design. This exhaustive module explores the low-level data structures that power modern databases, avoiding 'black box' thinking.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Select, tune, and justify database storage engines based on specific read/write workload patterns and enforce strict concurrency control.",
        "concepts": [
          "B-Trees: Mechanics of read-optimized storage",
          "LSM-Trees (Log-Structured Merge-Trees): MemTables, SSTables, and write-optimization",
          "Database Indexing at the binary level: Clustered vs Non-clustered",
          "Composite indexes and Covered queries",
          "Index maintenance overhead",
          "Transaction Theory beyond ACID",
          "Isolation Levels: Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable",
          "Concurrency phenomena: Dirty reads, Phantom reads",
          "MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) implementation",
          "NoSQL Paradigms: Key-Value, Document, Wide-Column, Graph data modeling"
        ],
        "sourceRefs": [
          "DDIA: Storage and Retrieval",
          "DDIA: Transactions"
        ],
        "prerequisites": [
          "SSD Internals: Page vs. Block writing",
          "Sequential vs. Random I/O economics"
        ]
      },
      "subtopics": [],
      "generated": false,
      "checkpoint": {
        "type": "quiz",
        "num_questions": 12,
        "pass_threshold": 85,
        "question_types": ["conceptual", "application", "true_false"]
      }
    },
    {
      "id": 4,
      "title": "Distributed Systems Theory: The Laws of Physics",
      "estimated_minutes": 720,
      "description": "Moving from one machine to hundreds introduces chaos. This module provides the mathematical and logical frameworks required to reason about distributed state and time.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Mathematically justify trade-offs between consistency and latency during network partitions, and architect robust consensus and clock-sync mechanisms.",
        "concepts": [
          "The Fallacies of Distributed Computing",
          "CAP Theorem: Proofs and practical limitations",
          "PACELC Theorem: Network partitioning vs Latency/Consistency tradeoffs",
          "Clock Synchronization: The myth of Wall Clock Time",
          "Logical Clocks: Lamport Clocks and Vector Clocks",
          "Consensus Algorithm Mechanics: Paxos",
          "Consensus Algorithm Mechanics: Raft (Leader Election and Log Replication)",
          "Quorum configurations and mathematics (R + W > N)",
          "Gossip Protocols for decentralized state synchronization"
        ],
        "sourceRefs": [
          "DDIA: The Trouble with Distributed Systems",
          "DDIA: Consistency and Consensus",
          "Raft: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Paper)"
        ],
        "prerequisites": [
          "Transaction Theory beyond ACID",
          "TCP Flow Control (Sliding Windows) and Congestion Control (Slow Start/Congestion Avoidance)"
        ]
      },
      "subtopics": [],
      "generated": false,
      "checkpoint": {
        "type": "quiz",
        "num_questions": 10,
        "pass_threshold": 80,
        "question_types": ["conceptual", "application", "scenario"]
      }
    },
    {
      "id": 5,
      "title": "Scalability Patterns & Distributed Architecture",
      "estimated_minutes": 600,
      "description": "Applying the theoretical laws to handle scale from 10 to 1,000,000 users. Focuses on the definitive routing, caching, and sharding algorithms used by hyperscalers.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Architect globally distributed systems utilizing consistent hashing, asynchronous message queues, and advanced caching without introducing data anomalies.",
        "concepts": [
          "Load Balancing: Layer 4 (Transport) vs Layer 7 (Application) deep dive",
          "Routing algorithms: Least Connections, IP Hash",
          "Consistent Hashing: Mathematical foundation of DHTs and cache distribution",
          "Caching Strategies: Cache Aside, Write-Through, Write-Behind",
          "Eviction Policies (LRU, LFU, ARC) and the Thundering Herd problem",
          "Database Sharding: Horizontal partitioning, Shard Keys, Hot Key mitigation",
          "Asynchronous Processing: Message Queue architectures (Kafka, RabbitMQ)",
          "Delivery Guarantees: At-least-once, At-most-once, Exactly-once delivery",
          "Event Sourcing and CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)",
          "Microservices vs. Cell-Based Architecture"
        ],
        "sourceRefs": [
          "AWS Architecture Blog: Cell-based architecture",
          "Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store (Paper)"
        ],
        "prerequisites": [
          "Quorum configurations and mathematics (R + W > N)",
          "NoSQL Paradigms: Key-Value, Document, Wide-Column, Graph data modeling"
        ]
      },
      "subtopics": [],
      "generated": false,
      "checkpoint": {
        "type": "quiz",
        "num_questions": 10,
        "pass_threshold": 80,
        "question_types": ["conceptual", "application", "scenario"]
      }
    },
    {
      "id": 6,
      "title": "Reliability, Observability & Defensive Design",
      "estimated_minutes": 360,
      "description": "A system is not truly designed unless its failure modes are mapped and mitigated. This module focuses on protecting the system from overload and ensuring transparent operations.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Implement self-healing architectural patterns, strict rate limiting, and comprehensive multi-layered observability to ensure 99.99%+ availability.",
        "concepts": [
          "Failure Mode mapping and 'Blast Radius' containment",
          "Circuit Breakers and state transitions (Open/Closed/Half-Open)",
          "Retries with Exponential Backoff and Jitter math",
          "Backpressure mechanics in high-throughput streams",
          "Rate Limiting Algorithms: Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Fixed/Sliding Window",
          "The Three Pillars of Observability",
          "Metrics: Time-series data modeling (Prometheus)",
          "Logging: Structured event aggregation (ELK Stack)",
          "Distributed Tracing: Request flow reconstruction (Jaeger/Zipkin)"
        ],
        "sourceRefs": [
          "Netflix Engineering Blog: Fault Tolerance"
        ],
        "prerequisites": [
          "Asynchronous Processing: Message Queue architectures (Kafka, RabbitMQ)",
          "Load Balancing: Layer 4 (Transport) vs Layer 7 (Application) deep dive"
        ]
      },
      "subtopics": [],
      "generated": false,
      "checkpoint": {
        "type": "quiz",
        "num_questions": 8,
        "pass_threshold": 80,
        "question_types": ["conceptual", "application", "calculation"]
      }
    },
    {
      "id": 7,
      "title": "Capstone Synthesis: Seminal Papers & Global Scale Architecture",
      "estimated_minutes": 600,
      "description": "The final integration. Reading the original academic papers that defined the industry, and applying all previous layers to design a global, Netflix-scale system.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Deconstruct seminal academic papers and architect a complete end-to-end global video streaming system justifying every hardware, network, and data tradeoff.",
        "concepts": [
          "Analysis: The Google File System (GFS) paper",
          "Analysis: Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System for Structured Data",
          "Analysis: MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters",
          "Architectural Application: Distributed Worker Queues for high-compute tasks (Transcoding)",
          "Architectural Application: Edge Delivery, CDNs, and Anycast DNS routing",
          "Architectural Application: Multi-region active-active database replication",
          "Synthesizing the stack: From epoll and LSM trees to Global Load Balancers",
          "Unlearning misconceptions: SQL global scaling (Spanner/CockroachDB)",
          "Throughput vs Latency optimization in the real world"
        ],
        "sourceRefs": [
          "The Morning Paper (Blog)",
          "The Google File System (Paper)",
          "Bigtable (Paper)",
          "MapReduce (Paper)"
        ],
        "prerequisites": [
          "Hardware & OS Primitives: The Physics of Software",
          "Network Internals & Transport Layer: The Synapses",
          "Storage Engine Mechanics: Data at Rest",
          "Distributed Systems Theory: The Laws of Physics",
          "Scalability Patterns & Distributed Architecture",
          "Reliability, Observability & Defensive Design"
        ]
      },
      "subtopics": [],
      "generated": false,
      "checkpoint": {
        "type": "quiz",
        "num_questions": 5,
        "pass_threshold": 100,
        "question_types": ["application", "scenario"]
      }
    }
  ]
}

parsed.goal: Professional mastery through first-principles engineering; building a bottom-up mental model of large-scale systems.

modules.title: Hardware & OS Primitives: The Physics of Software
modules.description: Before abstracting to the cloud, you must understand the uncompromising physical constraints of a single machine. This module establishes the absolute bedrock of system performance.

plan.goal: Calculate strict latency budgets and predict system bottlenecks by deeply understanding CPU, memory, and disk I/O primitives at the kernel level.
concepts: Memory hierarchy and nanosecond-level latency numbers
L1/L2/L3 Cache vs Main Memory vs Disk Seek
Blocking vs. Non-blocking I/O
Synchronous vs. Asynchronous I/O
Kernel-level event loops: epoll (Linux) and io_uring
CPU Concurrency vs. Parallelism
Context switching overhead and Kernel vs. User space transitions
Thread pools and user-space threads (Goroutines/Green threads)
SSD Internals: Page vs. Block writing
Sequential vs. Random I/O economics
sourceRefs: Latency Numbers Every Programmer Should Know
Designing Data-Intensive Applications (DDIA): Reliability, Scalability, and Maintainability

modules.title: Network Internals & Transport Layer: The Synapses
modules.description: Distributed systems are bound by the wires that connect them. This module strips away HTTP abstractions to build a first-principles understanding of how bytes actually move between machines.

plan.goal: Architect high-throughput, low-latency communication channels by manipulating transport protocols and serialization formats at the binary level.
concepts: TCP/IP Deep Dive: 3-way handshake mechanics
TCP Flow Control (Sliding Windows) and Congestion Control (Slow Start/Congestion Avoidance)
UDP fundamentals and limitations
QUIC protocol and HTTP/3: Solving Head-of-Line (HOL) blocking
HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 multiplexing mechanics
Binary Serialization Protocols: Protocol Buffers (Protobuf), Avro, Thrift
Schema evolution in distributed environments
gRPC multiplexing and streaming internals
WebSockets: Managing long-lived bidirectional state
REST: Statelessness and Idempotency guarantees
prerequisites: Memory hierarchy and nanosecond-level latency numbers

modules.title: Storage Engine Mechanics: Data at Rest
modules.description: Data is the only true state in system design. This exhaustive module explores the low-level data structures that power modern databases, avoiding 'black box' thinking.

plan.goal: Select, tune, and justify database storage engines based on specific read/write workload patterns and enforce strict concurrency control.
concepts: B-Trees: Mechanics of read-optimized storage
LSM-Trees (Log-Structured Merge-Trees): MemTables, SSTables, and write-optimization
Database Indexing at the binary level: Clustered vs Non-clustered
Composite indexes and Covered queries
Index maintenance overhead
Transaction Theory beyond ACID
Isolation Levels: Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable
Concurrency phenomena: Dirty reads, Phantom reads
MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) implementation
NoSQL Paradigms: Key-Value, Document, Wide-Column, Graph data modeling
prerequisites: SSD Internals: Page vs. Block writing

modules.title: Distributed Systems Theory: The Laws of Physics
modules.description: Moving from one machine to hundreds introduces chaos. This module provides the mathematical and logical frameworks required to reason about distributed state and time.

plan.goal: Mathematically justify trade-offs between consistency and latency during network partitions, and architect robust consensus and clock-sync mechanisms.
concepts: The Fallacies of Distributed Computing
CAP Theorem: Proofs and practical limitations
PACELC Theorem: Network partitioning vs Latency/Consistency tradeoffs
Clock Synchronization: The myth of Wall Clock Time
Logical Clocks: Lamport Clocks and Vector Clocks
Consensus Algorithm Mechanics: Paxos
Consensus Algorithm Mechanics: Raft (Leader Election and Log Replication)
Quorum configurations and mathematics (R + W > N)
Gossip Protocols for decentralized state synchronization
sourceRefs: DDIA: The Trouble with Distributed Systems
Raft: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Paper)
prerequisites: TCP Flow Control (Sliding Windows) and Congestion Control (Slow Start/Congestion Avoidance)

modules.title: Scalability Patterns & Distributed Architecture
modules.description: Applying the theoretical laws to handle scale from 10 to 1,000,000 users. Focuses on the definitive routing, caching, and sharding algorithms used by hyperscalers.

plan.goal: Architect globally distributed systems utilizing consistent hashing, asynchronous message queues, and advanced caching without introducing data anomalies.
concepts: Load Balancing: Layer 4 (Transport) vs Layer 7 (Application) deep dive
Routing algorithms: Least Connections, IP Hash
Consistent Hashing: Mathematical foundation of DHTs and cache distribution
Caching Strategies: Cache Aside, Write-Through, Write-Behind
Eviction Policies (LRU, LFU, ARC) and the Thundering Herd problem
Database Sharding: Horizontal partitioning, Shard Keys, Hot Key mitigation
Asynchronous Processing: Message Queue architectures (Kafka, RabbitMQ)
Delivery Guarantees: At-least-once, At-most-once, Exactly-once delivery
Event Sourcing and CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)
Microservices vs. Cell-Based Architecture
sourceRefs: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store (Paper)
prerequisites: Quorum configurations and mathematics (R + W > N)
NoSQL Paradigms: Key-Value, Document, Wide-Column, Graph data modeling

modules.title: Reliability, Observability & Defensive Design
modules.description: A system is not truly designed unless its failure modes are mapped and mitigated. This module focuses on protecting the system from overload and ensuring transparent operations.

plan.goal: Implement self-healing architectural patterns, strict rate limiting, and comprehensive multi-layered observability to ensure 99.99%+ availability.
concepts: Failure Mode mapping and 'Blast Radius' containment
Circuit Breakers and state transitions (Open/Closed/Half-Open)
Retries with Exponential Backoff and Jitter math
Backpressure mechanics in high-throughput streams
Rate Limiting Algorithms: Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Fixed/Sliding Window
The Three Pillars of Observability
Metrics: Time-series data modeling (Prometheus)
Logging: Structured event aggregation (ELK Stack)
Distributed Tracing: Request flow reconstruction (Jaeger/Zipkin)
prerequisites: Asynchronous Processing: Message Queue architectures (Kafka, RabbitMQ)
Load Balancing: Layer 4 (Transport) vs Layer 7 (Application) deep dive

modules.title: Capstone Synthesis: Seminal Papers & Global Scale Architecture
modules.description: The final integration. Reading the original academic papers that defined the industry, and applying all previous layers to design a global, Netflix-scale system.

plan.goal: Deconstruct seminal academic papers and architect a complete end-to-end global video streaming system justifying every hardware, network, and data tradeoff.
concepts: Analysis: The Google File System (GFS) paper
Analysis: Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System for Structured Data
Analysis: MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters
Architectural Application: Distributed Worker Queues for high-compute tasks (Transcoding)
Architectural Application: Edge Delivery, CDNs, and Anycast DNS routing
Architectural Application: Multi-region active-active database replication
Synthesizing the stack: From epoll and LSM trees to Global Load Balancers
Unlearning misconceptions: SQL global scaling (Spanner/CockroachDB)
Throughput vs Latency optimization in the real world
prerequisites: Hardware & OS Primitives: The Physics of Software
Network Internals & Transport Layer: The Synapses
Storage Engine Mechanics: Data at Rest
Distributed Systems Theory: The Laws of Physics
